skill.name: incident-runbook-templates
skill.description: Create incident response runbooks with triage steps, escalation paths, mitigation procedures, rollback guidance, and communication templates.
skill.summary: Generate practical incident runbooks for service, database, and production operations incidents.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, URL, device-file, IP, and weak-crypto patterns in SKILL.md. Review shows the command and network items are Markdown runbook examples, and the weak-crypto alerts are text-match false positives; the remaining risk is that users may copy disruptive operational commands without adapting them to their environment.

medium_findings.title: Operational Runbook Commands Require Review Before Use
medium_findings.description: The skill contains fenced Markdown examples for kubectl, psql, curl, and local scripts. These are not executed by the skill, but copying them into production can change deployments, terminate database sessions, alter feature flags, or run rollback scripts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly present in documentation examples and include production-changing operations. Confidence is high that this is a real operator safety concern, but not malicious code execution.

low_findings.title: Static Shell Execution Alerts Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The Ruby or shell backtick alerts correspond to Markdown fences, inline code, and example commands in SKILL.md. No executable Ruby code, shell wrapper, or automatic command invocation was found in the scanned file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged regions are visibly Markdown templates and fenced command examples. The repository file list contains only SKILL.md and no executable implementation file for automatic command execution.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Text Matches
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts appear to match ordinary text such as frontmatter description fields and SQL ORDER BY DESC examples. No cryptographic API, cipher selection, or hashing implementation was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are metadata or SQL sort clauses, not cryptographic code. There is no evidence of DES, MD5, SHA1, or another weak algorithm being used by the skill.

low_findings.title: Example URLs and Network Addresses in Templates
low_findings.description: The skill includes dashboard links, API health checks, Prometheus queries, external references, 0.0.0.0/0, and a private CIDR in example runbooks. These are documentation placeholders or operational examples, not automatic outbound requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are Markdown links, curl examples, and Kubernetes network policy examples. No code path sends data to these URLs automatically.

low_findings.title: Device File Access Is a curl Output Example
low_findings.description: The /dev/null reference is used in a curl command example to discard response output. It does not read sensitive files or write persistent data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited command uses /dev/null as a standard sink for curl output. This is a common diagnostic pattern and does not indicate file exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Operational Runbook Commands Require Review Before Use
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains fenced Markdown examples for kubectl, psql, curl, and local scripts. These are not executed by the skill, but copying them into production can change deployments, terminate database sessions, alter feature flags, or run rollback scripts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly present in documentation examples and include production-changing operations. Confidence is high that this is a real operator safety concern, but not malicious code execution.

content.value_statement: Incident teams need clear steps during outages. This skill drafts structured runbooks with triage, mitigation, rollback, verification, and communication sections.
actual_capabilities: Creates service outage runbooks with detection, triage, mitigation, rollback, and verification steps.
Builds database incident procedures for connection pools, replication lag, disk pressure, and query review.
Includes severity levels, escalation matrices, and stakeholder communication templates.
Provides example Kubernetes, Prometheus, curl, and SQL commands for common operational checks.
Guides post-incident improvement with testing, updates, assumptions, and postmortem practices.
limitations: It provides templates and examples, not environment-specific production validation.
Commands must be reviewed before use in live infrastructure.
It does not connect to monitoring, paging, or ticketing systems automatically.
It does not replace legal, security, or executive approval for major incidents.

use_cases.title: Prepare On-Call Service Runbooks
use_cases.description: Create repeatable procedures for engineers handling service outages, degraded dependencies, and rollback decisions.

use_cases.title: Standardize Database Incident Response
use_cases.description: Document how to triage connection exhaustion, replication lag, storage pressure, and long-running database work.

use_cases.title: Improve Incident Communications
use_cases.description: Draft internal updates, escalation paths, and resolution messages for consistent stakeholder communication.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Runbook
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an incident runbook for [service name]. Include severity, detection signals, first checks, escalation contacts, rollback steps, and verification.

prompt_templates.title: Adapt a Service Outage Template
prompt_templates.scenario: Customize the template for a production system.
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a service outage runbook for [service]. Use our stack: [technologies]. Include alerts, dashboards, triage commands, mitigation options, and communication updates.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Database Incident Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft a database incident runbook for [database type]. Cover connection pressure, replication lag, disk usage, slow queries, failover criteria, and recovery validation.

prompt_templates.title: Review and Harden a Runbook
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this incident runbook for gaps, unsafe commands, missing approvals, unclear escalation, weak verification, and post-incident follow-up. Suggest exact improvements.

output_examples.input: Create a payment service outage runbook.
output_examples.output: A structured runbook with impact assessment, alert checks, triage steps, mitigation paths, rollback guidance, verification checks, and stakeholder updates.

output_examples.input: Prepare for database connection pool exhaustion.
output_examples.output: A database response plan with connection review, long-running query checks, safe termination criteria, escalation points, and recovery validation.
Initial, update, and resolution message drafts with severity, impact, actions taken, owner, next steps, and follow-up fields.

best_practices: Replace placeholders with approved service names, contacts, dashboards, and escalation policies.
Test each runbook during game days before relying on it during an incident.
Require human review for commands that change production state or delete data.
anti_patterns: Copying example commands into production without checking namespace, service name, or blast radius.
Leaving escalation contacts, owners, or dashboards as generic placeholders.
Treating the template as a completed incident process without postmortem and review steps.

faq.question: Does this skill execute incident response commands?
faq.answer: No. It provides runbook text and command examples for humans to review and adapt.

faq.question: Can it create service-specific runbooks?
faq.answer: Yes. Provide the service name, architecture, alerts, dashboards, owners, and known dependencies.

faq.question: Is it useful during an active incident?
faq.answer: Yes, but use it as a drafting aid. Follow your approved incident process during live response.

faq.question: Can it cover database incidents?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes templates for connection issues, replication lag, disk pressure, and query review.

faq.question: What should teams review before publishing a runbook?
faq.answer: Review commands, permissions, escalation contacts, rollback criteria, verification steps, and customer communication rules.

faq.question: Does it integrate with PagerDuty, Grafana, or Slack?
faq.answer: No. It can reference those tools in templates, but it does not call their APIs.
